2605.12748.title: Simulating Students or Sycophantic Problem Solving? On Misconception Faithfulness of LLM Simulators
2605.12748.paper_summary: Large language models (LLMs) can fluently generate student-like responses, making them attractive as simulated students for training and evaluating AI tutors and human educators. Yet such simulators are typically evaluated by output similarity to real students, not by whether they behave like students with coherent misconceptions during interaction. We introduce a controlled framework for evaluating misconception faithfulness, whether a simulator maintains a misconception-driven belief state and updates selectively when feedback addresses the underlying misconception. Central to our framework is a misconception-contrastive feedback protocol that compares targeted feedback against two controls: misaligned feedback (targeting a different but plausible misconception) and generic feedback (only identifying answer is wrong). We propose Selective Flip Score (SFS), which quantifies how much more often a simulator flips its answer under targeted feedback than under contrastive controls. Across seven LLMs (4B-120B), multiple datasets, and prompting strategies, simulators exhibit near-zero SFS, correcting their answers at similarly high rates regardless of feedback relevance. Further analyses reveal a sycophantic failure mode: models behave less like students with misconceptions but more like problem-solvers who treat any corrective signal as a cue to abandon the simulated belief and re-solve from internal knowledge. To address this, we develop a post-training pipeline spanning supervised fine-tuning (SFT), preference optimization, and reinforcement learning (RL) with an SFS-aligned reward; SFT yields notable gains up to +0.56, and SFS-aligned RL provides more consistent improvements than preference optimization. Our results establish misconception faithfulness as a challenging yet trainable property, motivating a shift from static output matching toward interactive, belief-aware student modeling.
2605.12748.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）能夠流利地生成類似學生的回應，使其成為訓練和評估 AI 導師及人類教育者的模擬學生的理想選擇。<lb/>然而，這些模擬器通常是根據與真實學生的輸出相似性來評估，而不是根據它們在互動中是否表現出具有一致性誤解的學生行為。<lb/>我們提出了一個控制框架來評估誤解的真實性，即模擬器是否維持以誤解為驅動的信念狀態，並在反饋針對潛在誤解時選擇性地更新。<lb/>我們框架的核心是一個誤解對比反饋協議，該協議將針對性反饋與兩個控制組進行比較：不對齊反饋（針對不同但合理的誤解）和通用反饋（僅指出答案錯誤）。<lb/>我們提出了選擇性翻轉分數（SFS），它量化了模擬器在針對性反饋下翻轉答案的頻率，與在對比控制下的頻率相比。<lb/>在七個 LLM（4B-120B）、多個數據集和提示策略中，模擬器表現出接近零的 SFS，無論反饋的相關性如何，都以相似的高比例修正其答案。<lb/>進一步的分析揭示了一種諂媚的失敗模式：模型的行為更像是解決問題者，而不是具有誤解的學生，將任何糾正信號視為放棄模擬信念並從內部知識重新解決的提示。<lb/>為了解決這個問題，我們開發了一個涵蓋監督微調（SFT）、偏好優化和強化學習（RL）及 SFS 對齊獎勵的後訓練管道；SFT 產生了高達 +0.56 的顯著增益，而 SFS 對齊的 RL 提供了比偏好優化更一致的改進。<lb/>我們的結果確立了誤解的真實性作為一個具有挑戰性但可訓練的特性，促使從靜態輸出匹配轉向互動的、以信念為導向的學生建模。
2605.12748.author: Heejin Do et.al.
2605.12748.authors: Heejin Do, Shashank Sonkar, Mrinmaya Sachan
